question: What pleased the manager above everything else was how we met the target. (What pleased the manager above everything else)
explanation: This clause acts as the subject of the sentence. It tells us what was pleasing the manager.

question: Children who work hard in school please their parents. (who work hard in school)
options: B. noun clause in apposition to ‘children’
explanation: This clause describes the noun "Children." Words that describe nouns are called adjectives.

question: The tenants totally disagreed with what the landlord said. (what the landlord said)
options: D. noun clause object of the preposition ‘with’
explanation: The clause follows the word "with." In grammar, a noun clause that follows a preposition is its object.

question: How the thief escaped will forever remain a mystery to the chief. (How the thief escaped)
options: B. noun clause subject of ‘will remain’
explanation: This clause is the subject of the verb "will remain." It tells us what is a mystery.

question: The learners were allowed to go home because it was weekend. (because it was weekend)
options: D. noun clause object of ‘were allowed’
explanation: This clause gives the reason why the learners could go home. Clauses that give reasons are adverb clauses.

question: Have you visited the shop where they sell furniture? (where they sell furniture)
explanation: This clause describes the specific "shop." Because it describes a noun, it is an adjective clause.

question: Before she accepted, she carefully considered the proposal. (Before she accepted)
explanation: This clause tells us when she considered the proposal. Clauses that tell us time are adverb clauses.

question: The villagers cannot understand why she left her parents. (why she left her parents)
explanation: This clause tells us what the villagers cannot understand. It acts as the object of the verb.

question: The fact that he wants to marry a working lady is clear to his friends. (that he wants to marry a working lady)
options: D. noun clause in apposition to ‘fact’
explanation: This clause explains the noun "fact." When a noun clause explains a noun right before it, it is in apposition.

question: What surprised me was how he missed the train. (What surprised me)
explanation: This clause is the subject of the sentence. It tells us the thing that did the surprising.

question: Apoche is the student whom we chose to represent us. (whom we chose to represent us)
explanation: This clause describes the noun "student." It tells us more about which student we are talking about.

question: I shall discuss this issue with you when you come back. (when you come back)
options: A. noun clause object of ‘shall discuss’
B. noun phrase object of ‘shall discuss’
D. noun clause in apposition to ‘you’
explanation: This clause tells us the time when the discussion will happen. This makes it an adverb clause.

question: The driver wanted to know if we could hire him. (if we could hire him)
explanation: This clause tells us what the driver wanted to know. It acts as the object of the verb.

question: The assumption is that the situation will improve soon. (that the situation will improve soon)
explanation: This clause follows the verb "is" and describes the subject "assumption." This is called a subject complement.

question: The path that passes through the grazing field is rarely used. (that passes through the grazing field)
options: A. noun phrase in apposition to ‘path’
C. noun clause in apposition to ‘path’
explanation: This clause tells us which "path" we are talking about. It describes a noun, so it is an adjective clause.

question: He begged that his friend’s life might be spared. (that his friend’s life might be spared)
explanation: This clause tells us exactly what he begged for. It is the object of the verb "begged."

question: I purchased the book for which you bid. (for which you bid)
options: B. noun clause object of preposition ‘for’
C. noun phrase object of preposition ‘for’
explanation: This clause describes the noun "book." It tells us which book was purchased.

question: Mary and I once met when we were at school. (when we were at school)
explanation: This clause tells us the time when the meeting happened. Adverb clauses can tell us about time.

question: How the thief was caught remains a big puzzle. (How the thief was caught)
explanation: This clause is the subject of the verb "remains." It is the thing that is a puzzle.

question: The match began once the rains had stopped. (once the rains had stopped)
explanation: This clause tells us when the match started. Because it tells us about time, it is an adverb clause.

question: That he will refuse the offer is very unlikely. (That he will refuse the offer)
explanation: This clause is the subject of the sentence. It identifies the thing that is unlikely.

question: The winner will be whoever scores the highest points. (whoever scores the highest points)
options: B. noun clause complement of ‘will be’
D. noun clause subject of ‘will be’
explanation: This clause follows the verb "will be" and identifies the winner. This is a noun clause complement.

question: He told Dalo why he works hard. (why he works hard)
explanation: This clause tells us what he told Dalo. It acts as the direct object of the verb "told."

question: The teacher punished us because we were late. (because we were late)
options: A. adverb clause of result modifying ‘punished’
B. adverb clause of reason modifying ‘punished’
explanation: This clause gives the reason for the punishment. It is an adverb clause of reason.

question: I am telling you this lest you should make a mistake. (lest you should make a mistake)
options: A. adverb clause of purpose modifying ‘telling’
B. adverb clause of result modifying ‘telling’
explanation: The word "lest" shows the purpose of the action (to avoid a mistake). This is an adverb clause of purpose.

question: That question is easier than I thought. (than I thought)
options: C. adverb clause of comparison modifying ‘easier’
D. adverb clause of result modifying ‘is’
explanation: This clause compares how easy the question is to what the person thought.

question: The sun was so hot that we sat in the shade. (that we sat in the shade)
options: A. adverb clause of reason modifying ‘hot’
B. adverb clause of result modifying ‘hot’
explanation: This clause tells us the result of the sun being very hot.

question: The news that the children were found safe was received with relief. (that the children were found safe)
options: C. noun clause in apposition to ‘news’
D. noun phrase subject of ‘was received’
explanation: This clause explains exactly what the "news" was. It is a noun clause in apposition.

question: Since you’re so clever, you can tell us the answer. (Since you’re so clever)
options: A. adverb clause of reason modifying ‘can tell’
B. adverb clause of time modifying ‘can tell’
C. noun clause subject of ‘can tell’
explanation: The word "Since" here gives the reason why the person should know the answer.

question: If I’m not at the rendezvous, go straight to the check-in desk. (go straight to the check-in desk)
options: A. This is the main clause.
B. This is an adverbial clause of condition.
C. This is a noun clause object of ‘if’.
D. This is an adjective clause qualifying ‘straight’.
explanation: This is the independent part of the sentence that can stand alone as a complete thought.

question: They moved to an apartment which proved to be expensive. (which proved to be expensive)
options: D. adverb clause of result modifying ‘apartment’
explanation: This clause describes the "apartment." Clauses that describe nouns are adjective clauses.

question: I suddenly remembered which keys to press. (which keys to press)
explanation: This clause tells us what was remembered. It acts as the object of the verb.

question: My great-uncle, whom I’ve never met, is coming to visit. (whom I’ve never met)
explanation: This clause gives extra information about "uncle" and is set off by commas.

question: Whoever broke into the apartment left fingerprints. (Whoever broke into the apartment)
options: A. nominal relative clause subject of ‘left’
explanation: This is a special type of noun clause (nominal relative) acting as the subject of the sentence.

question: The question is how we get inside without a key. (how we get inside without a key)
explanation: This clause follows the verb "is" and explains the "question." It is a subject complement.

question: I turned my head so that I could hear the birdsong. (so that I could hear the birdsong)
options: A. adverb clause of result modifying ‘turned’
B. adverb clause of purpose modifying ‘turned’
explanation: The phrase "so that" shows why the person turned their head. This is a purpose.

question: People tend to act irrationally when they’re very depressed. (when they’re very depressed)
options: A. adverb clause of time modifying ‘act’
D. adverb clause of reason modifying ‘act’
explanation: This clause tells us the time or situation when people act this way.

question: He behaves as one who is mad. (as one who is mad)
options: C. adverb clause of manner modifying ‘behaves’
D. adverb clause of comparison modifying ‘behaves’
explanation: This clause tells us how or in what manner he is behaving.

question: I waited for my friend until he came. (until he came)
options: A. adverb clause of time modifying ‘waited’
B. adverb clause of condition modifying ‘waited’
explanation: The word "until" shows how long the waiting lasted. This is a clause of time.

question: He worked hard in order that he might win the prize. (in order that he might win the prize)
options: A. adverb clause of result modifying ‘worked’
B. adverb clause of purpose modifying ‘worked’
explanation: "In order that" tells us the reason or goal he had for working hard.

question: I asked him what his name was. (what his name was)
explanation: This clause tells us what was asked. It is the direct object of the verb.

question: I don’t know where he slept. (where he slept)
options: B. noun clause object of ‘don’t know’
explanation: This clause answers the question "what" I don't know. It is a noun clause object.

question: Why he did that remains unknown. (Why he did that)
explanation: The entire clause is the subject of the sentence. It tells us what is unknown.

question: It appears it will rain. (it will rain)
explanation: This noun clause completes the meaning of the verb "appears" as its complement.

question: The man you sent to me has run away. (you sent to me)
options: A. noun clause in apposition to ‘man’
explanation: This clause describes the "man." It tells us more about who the man is.

question: Though he was late, he came at last. (Though he was late)
options: A. adverb clause of concession modifying ‘came’
B. adverb clause of condition modifying ‘came’
explanation: The word "Though" shows a contrast or exception. This is an adverb clause of concession.

question: If I were you, I would wait till tomorrow. (If I were you)
options: A. adverb clause of time modifying ‘wait’
B. adverb clause of condition modifying ‘would wait’
C. noun clause object of ‘would wait’
explanation: This clause sets a condition for waiting. It is an adverb clause of condition.

question: No sooner had I opened the door than the thief fled. (than the thief fled)
options: A. adverb clause of result modifying ‘opened’
B. adverb clause of time modifying ‘opened’
explanation: This clause is used with "No sooner" to show the time when one action followed another.

question: Whatever happens, keep calm. (Whatever happens)
options: A. adverb clause of concession modifying ‘keep’
B. adverb clause of time modifying ‘keep’
explanation: This clause shows that the result (keeping calm) stays the same no matter what.

question: He ran so fast that I could not overtake him. (that I could not overtake him)
options: A. adverb clause of result modifying ‘fast’
B. adverb clause of purpose modifying ‘ran’
explanation: This clause shows the result of him running very fast.
